prs.title: feat: add helper utilities file
add MazeRunner game using graph + BFS
prs.body: This commit introduces a new game called “Maze Runner” under the MazeRunner/ folder. The game is implemented in Python and uses a graph data structure (adjacency list) to represent a maze (2D grid), and the BFS algorithm to compute the shortest path from the start (‘S’) to the goal (‘G’). The player can navigate the maze in the console using W/A/S/D commands, avoiding walls (‘#’). After the player reaches the goal (or quits), the program prints the optimal shortest path overlayed on the maze with ‘*’. The README.md in the folder describes how to play and explains the underlying data structure and algorithm used. This contribution aligns with the repository’s theme of “Create a Game with any data structures and algorithms”.

prs.title: add basic data-science template for new contributors
prs.body: This contribution introduces a new scaffolding template under the kaggle_workflow folder to help onboarding new contributors and accelerate data-science workflows within the Elixpo ecosystem. The template includes a configurable config.yaml, a simple dataset_loader.py module for loading, inspecting and saving datasets, and a Jupyter notebook basic_notebook_template.ipynb with ready-to-fill sections for exploratory data analysis. This aligns with the project’s goal of making open and accessible tooling for the community, and provides a “good first contribution” friendly resource that can be extended for modelling, experimentation, and dataset sharing.

prs.title: Add clear() method to reset console content
prs.body: This update introduces a clear() method to the Console class, allowing users to clear the console's content programmatically. This feature enhances the flexibility of the console, enabling dynamic content management in web applications.

prs.title: It’s a fun, beginner-friendly Python program that lets users test the…
prs.body: …ir typing speed and accuracy directly in the terminal. The script randomly selects a sentence, times the user's input, and calculates both Words Per Minute (WPM) and Accuracy (%). It encourages contributors to practice programming and typing in a playful way.

prs.title: add featured projects section and badges to README
Added Password Strength Checker project
prs.body:
The Password Strength Checker is a simple Python program that evaluates the strength of a user’s password. It checks the password based on its length, the presence of digits, uppercase and lowercase letters, and special characters. Based on these criteria, the program classifies the password as Weak, Moderate, or Strong.

This project is designed for beginners who want to contribute to open-source through Hacktoberfest 2025. It helps users understand core Python concepts such as string handling, regular expressions, and conditional statements while creating a useful and practical tool.

To run the program, navigate to the folder projects/password_strength_checker and execute the script using the command:
python password_checker.py

After running the program, enter your password when prompted to instantly see its strength evaluation.

prs.title: add image metadata extractor & organizer script in Python
prs.body:
### Add Image Metadata Extractor & Organizer (Python)

This contribution adds a utility script `image_metadata_organizer.py` under
`python_scripts/yourusername_image_metadata/` that allows users to:

- Scan a directory of images (jpg, jpeg, png)  
- Extract EXIF metadata (date taken, camera make/model, GPS if available)  
- Generate a CSV report of metadata  
- Optionally organize images into subfolders by date (YYYY/MM)  
- Support dry-run mode to preview file moves

**Why this is a good fit:**
- Practical and useful for many users who manage photo collections  
- Shows non-trivial Python (file I/O, metadata parsing, error handling)  
- Beginner-friendly, easy to understand and extend  
- Has clear usage and documentation  

**Usage example:**
```bash
cd HACKTOBERFEST_25_Python
pip install pillow
python3 python_scripts/yourusername_image_metadata/image_metadata_organizer.py /path/to/photos --report photos.csv --organize --dry-run

prs.title: add Graphviz export utility for visualizing code graphs
prs.body:
This PR adds a new script `graph_export.py` that allows users to export the internal
code relationship graph to a `.dot` file compatible with Graphviz.

**Highlights:**
- Supports filters for node types (functions, classes, etc.)
- Supports filters for edge labels (calls, inherits, contains)
- Option to limit the number of exported nodes for large projects
- Outputs a valid DOT file usable with Graphviz, Gephi, or other visualization tools.

prs.title: add segment tree with lazy propagation
prs.body:
This PR introduces a new C++ solution file `segment_tree_lazy.cpp` under the `c++/` directory.  
The implementation supports range updates and range queries (sum) using a classic segment tree with lazy propagation.

**Key features:**
- Build from initial array in O(n).
- Range update (add a value to all elements in a subarray) in O(log n).
- Range sum query in O(log n).
- Clean code, documented methods, and example `main` usage.
- Handles large inputs (using `long long`).

**Why this is a good addition:**
- Range data structure problems are very common in competitive programming / DSA practice.
- Many problems require efficient updates + queries; segment tree with lazy is a core tool.
- This adds depth to the existing DSA collection, covering “range queries + updates” scenario.

prs.title: Add Merge Sort visualization component
prs.body: This contribution adds a Merge Sort visualization component to the AlgoVisualizer project. The component provides an interactive view of the Merge Sort algorithm, allowing users to visualize how an array is recursively divided and merged step by step. It demonstrates key concepts of divide-and-conquer algorithms and helps learners understand the sorting process intuitively. The component is built using React, follows the existing project structure, and can be easily extended for other sorting algorithms or datasets.

prs.title: Add beginner-friendly Python factorial calculator
prs.body: This Python program calculates the factorial of a given positive integer using a simple loop. It handles invalid input by checking for negative numbers and provides the factorial for valid input. This program is beginner-friendly and demonstrates basic concepts of loops and conditional statements.

prs.title: Add iterative implementation for singly linked list reversal
prs.body:
This contribution provides a complete implementation of one of the core interview and computer science challenges: iteratively reversing a singly linked list.

The C code includes:

1. Full Data Structure Definition: A simple Node struct.

2. Memory Management: Functions for createNode and freeList to ensure proper heap usage and prevent memory leaks.

3. Core Algorithm (reverseList): The iterative solution uses three pointers (prev, current, and next_node) to efficiently reverse the direction of the next pointers in O(n) time complexity with O(1) auxiliary space.

This is a clean, well-documented C example for anyone studying data structures or contributing to open-source algorithm libraries.

prs.title: phone number validation and formatting utility
prs.body:
Description
This pull request introduces a crucial new utility function, validate_and_format_phone(), aimed at standardizing and cleaning up phone numbers. The goal is to get messy, user-provided phone numbers into a single, reliable format: the E.164 international standard (e.g., +15551234567).

Why this is a big deal:
Think about how often you see phone numbers entered in different ways—with dashes, parentheses, or just local digits. In any application that sends SMS messages, uses APIs, or stores contact information, these inconsistent formats are a recipe for bugs. This tool is designed to be the first line of defense against that problem, making sure all phone numbers are clean and consistent before they're used or stored in a database.

What the code does:
The function uses a few simple steps to get the job done:

It strips out all non-digit characters, but smartly keeps a leading + if it exists.

It handles cases where a country code is missing by automatically prepending a configurable default (like +1 for the US and Canada).

It includes a basic sanity check to ensure the final number has a realistic length, filtering out clearly invalid entries.

In short, it's a small but mighty piece of code that solves a very common, frustrating problem for developers.
